title: Tax Preparation Services

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $7,400.9M / sectoral output (T30) $12,593.9M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $62,700 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541213); class <5: 15,428 firms, avg revenue/firm $162,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,410 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,632 firms, avg revenue/firm $421,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,901 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 915 firms, avg revenue/firm $786,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $123,017 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 436 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,867,025 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $292,189 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,812,650 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,535,680 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $208,711,769 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,663,392 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 40
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Interview-driven 1040 preparation using guided software (e.g., TaxSlayer, Drake, ProSeries) is the canonical AI-automatable task — the preparer already follows a decision tree the software provides. AI removes the marginal labor in asking the taxpayer questions and populating forms. Remaining 15% covers document-intake trust (physical document handling), EITC due-diligence (IRS Form 8867 preparer diligence that requires human judgment on eligibility claims), ITIN handling (identity verification), and refund-advance product counseling. Note: the provided 4-digit 5412 OEWS wage_share for 13-2082 Tax Preparers is only 4.2%, which is wildly unrepresentative of 541213 proper (where preparers are the dominant labor class). Revenue/employee of $62,700 vs $263,807 at 541211 confirms the workforce here is seasonal preparers, not credentialed accountants. Per prompt guidance, the hypothesized structure is used and flagged: tax preparers estimated at ~55% of wage bill in 541213 (hypothesis from prompt).

Local marketing, seasonal hiring, franchisee compliance, and multi-unit operations management are the owner's core activities. In franchise systems, the client file and brand belong to the franchisor, not the local owner — owner's value is in operations execution. Some reporting, scheduling, and client communication automates (~20%). Strategic and relationship functions do not. Hypothesis wage share ~17.5% (owner SDE largely outside QCEW wages, so this understates true owner economics).

Quality review and exception handling for seasonal preparers; AI review tools (e.g., ProSeries AI checks) compress review cycles. Human oversight for audit-trigger situations, amended returns, and client escalations remains. Estimated 40% of role tasks are now compressible by AI tools.

Appointment setting, document intake, status calls, and data entry are substantially automatable. Physical document receipt from unbanked/low-tech clients requires some human presence; 15% residual. This is the highest-automatable segment after preparers themselves.

breakdown_by_role.role: Long tail (IT, HR, marketing, other operational staff)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Very small tail — tax prep storefronts have minimal IT/HR infrastructure; seasonal model keeps overhead thin. Mixed exposure at 0.30.

pi_dist.rationale: Tax prep pricing is flat fee per return benchmarked against strong free alternatives: TurboTax free tier, VITA sites (free for low-income), formerly IRS Direct File (now killed for filing season 2026 per IRS November 2025 announcement — a reprieve for storefronts, though permanent status uncertain). In franchise systems, the client file is frequently owned by the franchisor brand (H&R Block, Jackson Hewitt), not the local operator — client relationship control sits above the acquirable unit. Independent preparers have direct client ownership but face intense price competition from free digital alternatives. Revenue decomposition: ~70-75% prep fees (highly price-sensitive; AI removes labor, passes savings to clients via competition), ~25-30% refund product revenue (RALs/refund transfers — less price-sensitive, client wants money NOW, but regulatorily exposed to CFPB and state AGs). Pricing-model deflation is the steepest in the accounting group: flat per-return fees against free alternatives is a structural price floor mechanism. pi_dist=0.35 reflects weak client-relationship control (franchisor ownership, free-alternative pressure) partially offset by the refund-product channel stickiness.
sources: NATP 2025 Fee Study: average 1040 base fee ~$236 for all preparers; non-credentialed ~$185
IRS Direct File killed for 2026 (Federal News Network, November 2025)
H&R Block franchise FDD disclosure on client file ownership

pi_moat.rationale: The PTIN requirement to prepare returns for compensation creates a nominal barrier, but PTIN requires no educational credential — anyone can obtain one. No licensure protection shields tax prep storefronts from AI substitution the way CPA licensure shields attest services. Physical storefront presence offers some access/trust value for low-income, EITC-eligible, unbanked, and immigrant (ITIN) clients who distrust online filing or need in-person assistance — this is the most durable segment. Refund-advance product creates switching friction for the cash-now customer segment. Weak overall moat: TurboTax Full Service, VITA, and generic AI already provide competitive alternatives at lower or zero cost; the product (1040 preparation) has minimal complexity barrier that increases with generalist AI capability. pi_moat=0.30 reflects the physical/access/trust moat for EITC/ITIN/refund-product clients only.
sources: IRS PTIN requirements documentation: no education requirement
VITA program: IRS-sponsored free filing for <$67K income
H&R Block 10-K FY2025: 8,714 company + franchise offices as of March 2025

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption estimate ~30% (franchise platforms H&R Block and Jackson Hewitt have AI embedded by platform default; tech-forward independent preparers adopting guided AI tools). From Thomson Reuters 2025: 21% enterprise-level GenAI in tax firms (blended with CPA firms); franchise platforms moving faster. Historical analog: tax software reached ~50% CPA firm adoption in ~10-12 years organically; but for 541213 the tools are already integrated into franchise platforms which represent a large share of the market. From 30% current to 50% at same ~8pp/year pace as CPA firms: ~2.5 additional years ≈ 2029 from 2026; but micro-firm and sole-proprietor indie preparers (~70% of n_total) lag. Balancing against franchise platform acceleration: central estimate 4 years (50% by ~2030). Direct File being killed for 2026 slightly dampens the urgency for independents to adopt AI, but Intuit and Drake already embed AI tools that preparers use regardless. Range: 2-6 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Wolters Kluwer 2025 Future Ready Accountant Report (North America subset) + NATP/Thomson Reuters data
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 40% of North American firms use AI-driven tax research; H&R Block and Intuit have added generative AI into their products. Adjusted downward to 30% for 541213 specifically, reflecting that franchise platform AI is already embedded (effectively 100% of H&R Block/JH corporate stores) but independent preparers lag; blended estimate 30% materially using AI in automatable prep functions.

historical_analogs.value: Tax software reached ~50% preparer use in ~10-12 years (organic); e-file reached 50% individual returns in 2005 (~19 years from 1986 pilot, partially mandated for preparers after 2011)
historical_analogs.source: IRS e-file history / E-file.com; IRS e-file preparer mandate 2011
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: E-filed returns crossed the 50 percent threshold in 2005 (19 years from 1986 pilot). E-file mandate for preparers filing >10 returns took effect 2011. Tax software (Drake, ProSeries, TaxSlayer) reached widespread adoption in professional tax prep by ~2005-2010.

owners_60plus_pct.source: IRS PTIN holder statistics / gitnux.org tax preparation industry statistics (2026 compilation)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Preparers aged 45-64 comprise 52% of the workforce; average experience 12 years. No specific owner-age data for 541213 operators separately published. Estimate of 0.30 (30% of owners aged 60+) based on preparer age distribution and typical small-business owner demographics — flagged as ESTIMATE.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No documented succession shortage specific to NAICS 541213. Franchise systems (H&R Block, Jackson Hewitt, Liberty Tax) provide structural continuity — the brand and client file survive owner departure. Independent preparers have high turnover (18% annual). No NATP or IRS data documents a succession planning gap analogous to the AICPA-documented CPA shortage. Not documented.

active_consolidators.source: CPA Trendlines / franchise industry data; BizBuySell 2025 buyer pool report
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active 2026 buyer pool includes H&R Block (franchise acquisition program), Jackson Hewitt (PE-backed by Corsair Capital), Liberty Tax (NASDAQ: TAX/NextPoint Financial). No dedicated PE accounting platform actively accumulating NAICS 541213 at scale — n_band of only 40 firms makes this unattractive for PE platform formation. Count 3 active consolidators.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions tax preparation business valuation guide (2026); BizBuySell accounting/tax practice benchmarks 2025
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Seasonal tax prep practices: 0.75-1.1x revenue; 2.5-4x SDE. EBITDA normalization applied per Rule 3: seasonal prep firm at ~20-25% normalized EBITDA margin after market-rate replacement owner compensation → 0.9x revenue implies ~3.5-4.5x normalized EBITDA. BizBuySell 2025: average earnings multiple 2.34 (SDE-based); converting to normalized EBITDA (lower than SDE by the add-back gap) yields ~3.0-4.0x. Central estimate 3.5x normalized EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: H&R Block public company EV/EBITDA; franchise industry comparable data
exit_mult.figure_quoted: H&R Block (HRB) trades at approximately 3.5-4x EBITDA as a public company (market cap ~$3.5B, EBITDA ~$976M as of FY2025). No private platform exits disclosed for Jackson Hewitt (Corsair Capital). No dedicated PE tax prep platform exit at the CPA-firm PE scale. Exit_mult of 6.0x reflects a modest premium for multi-unit regional operators above the H&R Block public comp, acknowledging secular headwinds cap the ceiling. Quality LOW — very limited comparable transaction data for this specific segment at platform scale.

pricing_structure.value: Flat fee per return (~70-75% of revenue); refund product revenue (~25-30%: refund advances, refund transfers). NATP 2025: average base 1040 fee ~$236 all preparers, ~$185 non-credentialed. Revenue ~75-85% earned January-April. IRS Direct File was killed for 2026 filing season (IRS November 2025 announcement) — a temporary reprieve from free government competition.
pricing_structure.source: NATP 2025 Tax Professional Fee Study; IRS Direct File status (Federal News Network November 2025)
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Average base fee for Form 1040 with Schedules 1-3 rose 45.7% from $162 in 2024 to $236 in 2026 across all preparer types; non-credentialed preparers ~$185. IRS Direct File 'will not be available in Filing Season 2026' per IRS communication to states (Federal News Network, November 2025).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw=0.3982 implies 39.8 pp of revenue potentially freed by AI — the highest uplift in the scoring set, driven by the extremely high ai_replaceable_share (0.6775) for this labor-intensive, task-routine workforce. A=2.5 (t50=4 years), below 5, so the uplift<3pp tension check does not trigger. The high V (10.0, capped) and low C (1.05) reflect the fundamental tension in this industry: AI can automate almost all the work, but the operator captures very little of the savings due to competitive price pressure and franchisor client-file ownership. This is consistent: high V means there is enormous theoretical AI value; low C means it flows to clients and the franchisor, not the acquired unit's EBITDA.

terminal_value.justification: Simple 1040 tax return preparation is the most AI-terminal paid service in the scoring set. The product (guided question-and-answer return completion) is being replaced, not just automated internally. Evidence: (1) Intuit TurboTax Full Service and H&R Block AI Assist already deliver near-complete guided filing with AI; (2) IRS Direct File (now killed for 2026 by political/DOGE action, but structurally viable and may return under future administrations) offers free government-sponsored filing; (3) the PTIN-holder preparer follows the same decision tree the software already provides — there is no licensed judgment layer protecting this work. Durable residual: EITC due-diligence (IRS Form 8867 requires preparer attestation — a thin human layer), ITIN processing (identity verification in person), refund-advance product distribution (money product, not a tax service per se), and non-filer resolution/back-tax situations. These residuals are estimated at 15-25% of current revenue. The majority of storefront revenue (simple 1040 prep for W-2/standard-deduction filers) has no structural protection against AI + free alternatives. Classified MELTING: the product itself is being replaced for the modal customer, not just the cost structure of delivering it. Note: DOGE killing Direct File for 2026 delays but does not prevent the MELTING trajectory.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V=10.0 was derived from BLS labor_share (provided dataset) × ai_replaceable_share computed from the hypothesized role structure (per prompt instruction for this code, given the 4-digit OEWS is unrepresentative). Within-role automatable fractions were justified by task-level analysis of seasonal prep work (decision-tree prep, document intake) — no deal activity referenced. A=2.5 was derived from current adoption estimate (~30% franchise + forward-leaning indie) and historical tax-software/e-file analog — no consolidation trends used.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing structure: flat fee per return for ~70-75% of revenue; refund-product revenue (~25-30%) is fee-per-product. The flat-per-return model against free alternatives (TurboTax free, VITA, formerly Direct File) is the steepest deflation setup: when AI removes the labor from preparation, competitive pressure and free alternatives force per-return fees toward zero for simple returns. The W-2/standard-deduction 1040 is already priced near the marginal cost of the labor at ~$185 for non-credentialed preparers; when AI removes that labor cost, the competitive floor collapses. Refund products (RALs, refund transfers) are less price-sensitive — they're financial products with embedded interest/fees that customers pay for immediate cash access. pi_dist=0.35 and pi_moat=0.30 jointly reflect: (a) the franchisor owns the client file in franchise systems (reducing acquirer's pi_dist), (b) no licensure barrier protects prep fees (reducing pi_moat), (c) refund product provides partial pricing insulation. C=1.05 is the lowest C in the scoring set, reflecting that almost none of the V=10.0 AI value converts to operator EBITDA — it passes to clients as lower fees or is retained by the franchisor.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2: A=5.0, S=4.37; at t50=4 (central): A=2.5, S=3.19; at t50=6: A=1.67, S=2.77. Range of ~1.6 S-points — highest sensitivity of any input.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.60-0.75 (varies within-role fractions for preparers 0.75-0.92 and reception 0.70-0.92)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At share=0.60: V_raw=0.5877×0.60=0.3526, V=10.0 (still capped); at share=0.75: V_raw=0.5877×0.75=0.4408, V=10.0 (still capped). V is insensitive to ai_replaceable_share variation because V is already at the cap (10.0). Score impact minimal from share changes — all plausible range values exceed 0.25/labor_share=0.425 threshold.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 2.5-5.0x normalized EBITDA (wide spread: SDE-based broker multiples 2.34x average earnings vs normalized EBITDA after replacement owner comp)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At buy=2.5 (exit=6.0): M=clamp(4×3.5/2.5,0,10)=clamp(5.6,0,10)=5.6, S=4.05; at buy=3.5 (central): M=2.857, S=3.19; at buy=5.0 (exit=6.0): M=clamp(4×1.0/5.0,0,10)=0.80, S=2.21. Range ~1.8 S-points — buy_mult is highly influential.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.25-0.45 (lower if franchisor file ownership dominates; higher if refund-product stickiness is primary)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At pi_dist=0.25 (pi_moat=0.30): C=0.75, S=2.90; at pi_dist=0.45 (pi_moat=0.30): C=1.35, S=3.42. Range ~0.5 S-points.

reviewer_note: Role-mix critical flag: The provided OEWS role_mix is 4-digit 5412, dominated by CPA-firm occupations (13-2011 Accountants at 34.2% of wages). This is wildly unrepresentative of NAICS 541213 where seasonal preparers (13-2082 Tax Preparers) are the dominant labor class. The SUSB revenue/employee of $62,700 at 541213 vs $263,807 at 541211 confirms a low-wage, seasonal workforce inconsistent with the provided OEWS structure. Per prompt instruction for this code, a hypothesized role structure was used (seasonal preparers ~55%, owners ~17.5%, managers ~10%, reception ~15%, long tail ~2.5%), flagged as LOW quality for ai_replaceable_share. The high ai_replaceable_share (0.6775) reflects the extremely routine, algorithm-guided nature of consumer 1040 preparation — not an artifact of the provided OEWS structure. EBITDA normalization: broker-quoted multiples for tax prep practices are SDE-based (owner comp inside); buy_mult of 3.5x is normalized to post-replacement-owner-comp EBITDA per Rule 3. Exit_mult of 6.0x is sourced primarily from H&R Block public company comparables — a weak comp for private platform exits; flagged LOW quality. IRS Direct File cancellation (November 2025): reduces near-term substitution threat but does not change the structural MELTING classification — the underlying technology (Intuit, Google, OpenAI) continues developing regardless of government program status. The MELTING terminal classification drives a cap consideration: if thresholds.json caps MELTING codes, S may be capped below 3.19. Terminal value MELTING reflects the modal customer segment (W-2/standard deduction 1040) facing product substitution; complex/EITC/ITIN/refund-product segments are more durable.
